title: Probability with permutations and combinations
description: Use permutations and combinations to find probabilities.
topic_path: Topic A: Lesson 4: Using permutations and combinations to compute probabilities

assessment_items.question_content:
Declan's friend Luka claims that he can read minds. To test Luka's abilities, Declan draws $5$ cards *without replacement* from a standard deck of $52$ playing cards. Declan then asks Luka to identify *in any order* which $5$ cards he drew  without looking.

Assume that Luka has no special abilities and is randomly guessing the cards.

**What is the probability that Luka correctly identifies all $5$ cards in any order?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of correct arrangements}}{\# \text{ of ways to draw }5 \text{ cards}}$

Since we're drawing without replacement and each of the cards is distinct, we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

Declan asked Luka to identify *in any order* which $5$ cards he drew. That means Luka doesn't need to worry about matching the exact order, and so *order doesn't matter*.

So we should use **combinations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order doesn't matter, there are $_{52} \, \text{C} \, _5$ total ways to select $5$ cards from a deck of $52$ cards.

Luka is trying to identify all $5$ of the $5$ cards (in any order) that Declan drew. Although we could, we don't need to use combinations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible sets of $5$ cards is the correct set.

$P=\dfrac{1}{_{52} \, \text{C} \, _5}$

###Answer

The probability that Luka correctly identifies all $5$ cards in any order:

$\dfrac{1}{_{52} \, \text{C} \, _5}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Lin and Kai are friends that work together on a team of $12$ total people. Their manager is going to randomly select $2$ people from the team of $12$ to attend a conference.

**What is the probability that Lin and Kai are the $2$ people chosen?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of groups with Lin and Kai}}{\# \text{ of total groups}}$

Since the manager wants to send $2$ people, they must be selecting without replacement so they don't get the same person twice. So we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability Lin and Kai are the $2$ people chosen. Regardless of the order they are selected, both people chosen will attend the conference, so *order doesn't matter*.

So we should use **combinations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order doesn't matter, there are $_{12} \, \text{C} \, _2$ total ways to select $2$ people from a group of $12$ people.

We want to count how many of those groups contain Lin and Kai. Although we could, we don't need to use combinations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible groups of $2$ people is Lin and Kai.

$P=\dfrac{1}{_{12} \, \text{C} \, _2}$

###Answer

The probability that Lin and Kai are the $2$ people chosen:

$\dfrac{1}{_{12} \, \text{C} \, _2}$

###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of groups with sausage and onion}}{\# \text{ of total groups}}$

Since Rosa's mom is choosing $2$ different toppings, she must be selecting without replacement. So we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability that Rosa's mom chooses sausage and onion. Regardless of the order they are selected, both toppings chosen will be on the pizza, so *order doesn't matter*.

So we should use **combinations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order doesn't matter, there are $_{8} \, \text{C} \, _2$ total ways to select $2$ toppings from a list of $8$ toppings.

We want to count how many of those groups contain sausage and onion. Although we could, we don't need to use combinations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible groups of $2$ toppings is sausage and onion.

$P=\dfrac{1}{_{8} \, \text{C} \, _2}$

###Answer

The probability that Rosa's mom chooses sausage and onion:

$\dfrac{1}{_{8} \, \text{C} \, _2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Tobin's friend Rose claims that she can read minds. To test Rose's abilities, Tobin draws $3$ cards *without replacement* from a standard deck of $52$ playing cards. Tobin asks Rose to identify each of the $3$ cards she drew *in order*.

Assume that Rose has no special abilities and is randomly guessing the cards.

**What is the probability that Rose identifies all $3$ cards in the correct order?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of correct arrangements}}{\# \text{ of ways to draw }3 \text{ cards}}$

Since we're drawing without replacement and each of the cards is distinct, we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

Tobin asked Rose to identify each of the $3$ cards she drew *in order*, so *order matters*.

So we should use **permutations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order matters, there are $_{52} \, \text{P} \, _3$ total ways to select $3$ cards from a deck of $52$ cards.

Rose is trying to identify all $3$ of the cards in the correct order. We don't need to use permutations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible orders is the correct order.

$P=\dfrac{1}{_{52} \, \text{P} \, _3}$

###Answer

The probability that Rose identifies all $3$ cards in the correct order:

$\dfrac{1}{_{52} \, \text{P} \, _3}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Adina sets up a taste test of $3$ different waters: tap, bottled in glass, and bottled in plastic. She puts these waters in identical cups and has a friend taste them one by one. The friend then tries to identify which water was in each cup.

Assume that Adina's friend can't taste any difference and is randomly guessing.

**What is the probability that Adina's friend correctly identifies each of the $3$ cups of water?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of correct sequences}}{\# \text{ of possible sequences}}$

Since Adina's friend won't be repeating guesses and each of the options is distinct, we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

Adina's friend needs to identify each of the cups correctly. That means they need to identify the cups *in the correct order*, so *order matters*.

So we should use **permutations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order matters, there are $_{3} \, \text{P} \, _3$ total ways to arrange the $3$ cups of water.

The friend is trying to identify all $3$ of the cups in the correct order. We don't need to use permutations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible sequences is correct.

$P=\dfrac{1}{_{3} \, \text{P} \, _3}$

###Answer

The probability that Adina's friend correctly identifies each of the $3$ cups of water:

$\dfrac{1}{_{3} \, \text{P} \, _3}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A passcode to enter a building is a sequence of $4$ single digit numbers $(0$—$9)$, and repeated digits aren't allowed.

Suppose someone doesn't know the passcode and randomly guesses a sequence of $4$ digits.

**What is the probability that they guess the correct sequence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of correct sequences}}{\# \text{ of possible sequences}}$

Since repeated digits aren't allowed, we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability that they guess the correct sequence. That means they need to identify the digits *in the correct order*, so *order matters*.

So we should use **permutations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order matters, there are $_{10} \, \text{P} \, _4$ total ways to select $4$ digits without repeats from $10$ digits.

Someone is trying to identify all $4$ of the digits in the correct order. We don't need to use permutations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible sequences is the correct sequence.

$P=\dfrac{1}{_{10} \, \text{P} \, _4}$

###Answer

The probability that they guess the correct sequence:

$\dfrac{1}{_{10} \, \text{P} \, _4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Each card in a standard deck of $52$ playing cards is unique and belongs to $1$ of $4$ suits:

- $13$ cards are clubs
- $13$ cards are diamonds
- $13$ cards are hearts
- $13$ cards are spades

Suppose that Luisa randomly draws $4$ cards *without replacement*.

**What is the probability that Luisa gets $2$ diamonds and $2$ hearts (in any order)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of ways to get }2 \text{ diamonds AND }2 \text{ hearts}}{\# \text{ of ways to draw }4 \text{ cards}}$

Since we're drawing without replacement and each of the cards is distinct, we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability that Luisa gets $2$ diamonds and $2$ hearts *in any order*, so *order doesn't matter*.

So we should use **combinations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order doesn't matter, there are $_{52} \, \text{C} \, _4$ total ways to select $4$ cards from a deck of $52$ cards.

We want to count all of ways we can pair $2$ diamonds with $2$ hearts (again, order doesn't matter):

- There are $_{13} \, \text{C} \, _2$ total ways to select $2$ diamonds from $13$ total diamonds.
- There are $_{13} \, \text{C} \, _2$ total ways to select $2$ hearts from $13$ total hearts.

So there are $\left(_{13} \, \text{C} \, _2\right) \cdot \left(_{13} \, \text{C} \, _2\right)$ total ways we can pair $2$ diamonds with $2$ hearts.

$P=\dfrac{\left(_{13} \, \text{C} \, _2\right) \cdot \left(_{13} \, \text{C} \, _2\right)}{_{52} \, \text{C} \, _4}$

###Answer

The probability that Luisa gets $2$ diamonds and $2$ hearts (in any order):

$\dfrac{\left(_{13} \, \text{C} \, _2\right) \cdot \left(_{13} \, \text{C} \, _2\right)}{_{52} \, \text{C} \, _4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Micah is $1$ of $28$ students in a class. Micah's teacher is going to randomly select $3$ students from their class to visit a classroom of younger students.

**What is the probability Micah is included in the group of students chosen?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ groups that include Micah}}{\# \text{ of possible groups}}$

Since the teacher wants a group of $3$ students, they must be selecting without replacement so they don't get the same student twice. So we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability Micah is included in the group of students chosen. Regardless of the order they are selected, all $3$ students will visit the other classroom, so *order doesn't matter*.

So we should use **combinations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order doesn't matter, there are $_{28} \, \text{C} \, _3$ total ways to select $3$ students from a class of $28$ students.

We want to count all of the groups that include Micah. This is equivalent to how many groups of $2$ students are possible from his $27$ classmates. Micah could then "join" any of those groups, and we have counted how many groups of $3$ students include Micah. 

Again, order doesn't matter, so there are $_{27} \, \text{C} \, _2$ groups that include Micah.

$P=\dfrac{_{27} \, \text{C} \, _2}{_{28} \, \text{C} \, _3}$

###Answer

The probability Micah is included in the group of students chosen:

$\dfrac{_{27} \, \text{C} \, _2}{_{28} \, \text{C} \, _3}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Nia is $1$ of $24$ students in a class. Every month, Nia's teacher randomly selects $4$ students from their class to act as class president, vice president, secretary, and treasurer. No one student can hold two positions.

**In a given month, what is the probability that Nia is chosen as president?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of arrangements with Nia as president}}{\# \text{ of possible arrangements}}$

Since no one student can hold two positions, the teacher must be selecting without replacement so they don't get the same student twice. So we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability that Nia is chosen as president. Just being selected in the group of $4$ students isn't enough in this scenario — *the position for which they get selected also matters*, so *order matters*.

So we should use **permutations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order matters, there are $_{24} \, \text{P} \, _4$ total ways to select $4$ students for these positions from a class of $24$ students.

We want to count all of the arrangements that include Nia as president. This is equivalent to how many arrangements of $3$ students are possible from Nia's $23$ classmates. Nia could then "join" any of those arrangements as president, and we have counted how many arrangements of $4$ students include Nia as president. 

Again, order matters, so there are $_{23} \, \text{P} \, _3$ arrangements that include Nia as president.

$P=\dfrac{_{23} \, \text{P} \, _3}{_{24} \, \text{P} \, _4}$

###Answer

The probability that Nia is chosen as president:

$\dfrac{_{23} \, \text{P} \, _3}{_{24} \, \text{P} \, _4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Rosa's friend Jake claims that he can read minds. To test Jake's abilities, Rosa chooses $3$ different numbers from $1$ to $10$. Rosa then asks Jake to identify which $3$ of the $10$ numbers she chose *in the correct order*.

Assume that Jake has no special abilities and is randomly guessing the numbers.

**What is the probability that Jake correctly identifies all $3$ numbers in the correct order?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of correct sequences}}{\# \text{ of possible sequences}}$

Since Rosa is choosing $3$ different numbers, we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability that Jake guesses the correct sequence. That means Jake needs to identify the numbers *in the correct order*, so *order matters*.

So we should use **permutations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order matters, there are $_{10} \, \text{P} \, _3$ total ways to select $3$ numbers without repeats from $10$ numbers.

Jake is trying to identify all $3$ of the numbers in the correct order. We don't need to use permutations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible sequences is the correct sequence.

We can calculate $\maroonD{_{10} \, \text{P} \, _3}$ directly with most calculators, or we can use the permutations formula:

$P=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{_{10} \, \text{P} \, _3}}=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{720}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The probability that Jake correctly identifies all $3$ numbers in the correct order:

$\dfrac{1}{720}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Michael is taking a quiz in his music history class. The teacher writes the names of $6$ songs on the board, and then plays $4$ songs out of the $6$, one after the other in a random sequence. Michael then needs to identify each of the songs *in the sequence they were played*.

Suppose that Michael hasn't studied and is randomly guessing the songs.

**What is the probability that Michael correctly identifies all $4$ songs in the correct order?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of correct sequences}}{\# \text{ of possible sequences}}$

Since Michael is choosing $4$ different songs, we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability that Michael correctly identifies all $4$ songs *in the correct order*, so *order matters*.

So we should use **permutations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order matters, there are $_{6} \, \text{P} \, _4$ total ways to arrange $4$ songs without repeats from a set of $6$ songs.

Michael is trying to identify all $4$ songs in the correct order. We don't need to use permutations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible sequences is the correct sequence.

We can calculate $\maroonD{_{6} \, \text{P} \, _4}$ directly with most calculators, or we can use the permutations formula:

$P=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{_{6} \, \text{P} \, _4}}=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{360}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The probability that Michael correctly identifies all $4$ songs in the correct order:

$\dfrac{1}{360}$

###Strategy

Our probability will look something like this:

$P=\dfrac{\# \text{ of correct sets}}{\# \text{ of possible sets}}$

Since Sofie is choosing $3$ different ingredients, we can use permutations and combinations to help us find the counts in the numerator and/or denominator. The most important thing is to distinguish whether or not order matters in the given scenario:

- If order matters, we use **permutations**.
- If order doesn't matter, we use **combinations**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Permutations or combinations?

We want the probability that Sofie correctly identifies the set of $3$ ingredients in the cake. She doesn't need to identify an order of any sort, so *order doesn't matter*.

So we should use **combinations** to get the counts in the numerator and denominator.

###Counts

If order doesn't matter, there are $_{6} \, \text{C} \, _3$ total ways to choose $3$ ingredients without repeats from a set of $6$ ingredients.

Sofie is trying to identify the correct set of $3$ ingredients. We don't need to use combinations to count here — exactly $1$ of the total possible sets is the correct set.

We can calculate $\maroonD{_{6} \, \text{C} \, _3}$ directly with most calculators, or we can use the combinations formula:

$P=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{_{6} \, \text{C} \, _3}}=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{20}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The probability that Sofie correctly identifies the set of $3$ ingredients in the cake:

$\dfrac{1}{20}$
